one_line_takeaway: A GRPO-based, reward-model-free RL recipe whose vision-grounded reward (format+recall+precision) plus a curriculum that tightens thresholds mid-training boosts 7B LVLM object localization by up to ~9 mAP and generalizes better than SFT — an alignment/CV contribution, not a security one.
key_finding: Vision-R1 consistently improves localization, with the largest gains on the initially weaker model: Qwen2.5-VL-7B COCO mAP rises from 17.7 to 26.6 (+8.9, ~50% relative) and ODINW-13 avg mAP from 37.0 to 46.0 (+9.0), surpassing the 10x-larger Qwen2.5-VL-72B (43.1 ODINW). Griffon-G-7B gains are smaller (mAP 40.2->42.0, +1.8; ODINW 43.8->46.3, +2.5) since it starts strong. Crucially, RL generalizes far better than SFT: on out-of-domain ODINW subsets Vision-R1 adds +7.1 (Griffon-G) and +4.8 (Qwen) while SFT is roughly flat or worse, and SFT actually degrades Qwen2.5-VL-7B's ODINW-13 (37.0->35.0) and general QA, whereas Vision-R1 preserves QA performance. The progressive refinement matters: switching to the advanced phase at STEP=1/2 gives best Griffon-G COCO mAP (42.1) versus 39.9 with no adjustment (below the 40.2 baseline).
core_contribution: Vision-R1 is a vision-guided, R1-like (GRPO-based) reinforcement learning algorithm for post-training Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) on object-localization tasks, without any human-annotated preference data or trained reward model. Its two core components are (1) a criteria-driven reward function that converts textual coordinate outputs into visual feedback and scores each completion on three axes — a binary dual-format reward (template + numerical content validity), a recall reward (fraction of ground-truth objects validly predicted above an IoU threshold), and a precision reward (mean IoU of valid predictions) — and (2) a progressive rule refinement strategy that dynamically tightens reward criteria over training via a 'differentiation' mapping (penalize low recall/IoU, saturate high ones) and 'staged progression' (an easier beginner phase with IoU/reward thresholds 0.5/0.5/0.75 that switches to a stricter advanced phase at 0.75/0.75/0.9). The paper's OWN contribution is this reward design plus the curriculum-style refinement; GRPO itself and the DeepSeek-R1 rule-based-RL paradigm it extends are prior work (cited [17,38]), not novel here.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a vision-language RL/alignment paper focused on object-localization accuracy and generalization, not on an adversary. The only security-adjacent notion is 'reward hacking,' but the paper treats it purely as a training-stability/optimization pathology (the policy exploiting a static reward to emit many low-quality boxes) that the progressive rule refinement mitigates — there is no attacker, threat surface, or defense evaluation. No threat model should be inferred.
limitations: Scope is narrow: only object-localization tasks and only two 7B base models are studied; all experiments are 7B (no scaling study). The out-of-domain protocol is relaxed — the authors explicitly do NOT require both images and categories to be unseen, only that either the category or scene is absent, weakening the 'generalization' claim. The optimal refinement transition point is model-dependent and hand-tuned (STEP=1/2 best for Griffon-G but STEP=1 best for Qwen2.5-VL-7B, per their own text), indicating the curriculum schedule needs per-model tuning rather than being principled. The reward-component ablation folds in a substituted 'binary count reward' when recall is removed, so it is not a clean leave-one-out. Reported deltas on some ODINW subsets are tiny/noisy (e.g., ThermalCheetah drops under Vision-R1). No statistical significance or seed variance is reported.

datasets_used: MSCOCO val2017 (in-domain detection eval + 30K training detection samples)
ODINW-13 (13 real-world detection settings, grounding eval)
ODINW non-overlapping subsets (out-of-domain: BoggleBoards, MountainDewCommercial, ThermalCheetah, Vector)
V3Det (>13K categories, used for visual grounding training)
RefCOCO and GRefCOCO / Visual Genome (REC training)
GQA, AI2D, ChartQA, SEED (general VQA eval)
Curated 49K RL training set: 30K detection, 9K grounding, 10K REC
quantitative_results: Qwen2.5-VL-7B ODINW-13 avg mAP: 37.0 (base) -> 35.0 (SFT) -> 46.0 (Vision-R1, +9.0), exceeding Qwen2.5-VL-72B's 43.1
Out-of-domain avg mAP (Griffon-G): 12.2 base -> 12.4 SFT -> 19.3 Vision-R1 (+7.1); (Qwen): 16.9 -> 17.9 -> 21.7 (+4.8)
Reward-component ablation (Griffon-G COCO): baseline mAP 40.2 -> precision-only 41.5 -> precision+recall 42.1
Progressive refinement STEP ablation (Griffon-G COCO mAP): baseline 40.2, STEP 1/3 = 41.5, STEP 1/2 = 42.1 (best), STEP 1 (no adjustment) = 39.9
Matcher ablation: box-only mAP 42.1 / AR100 54.2 vs box+label 41.9 / 53.4
General QA preserved (Griffon-G): GQA 64.6->64.8, AI2D 70.1->70.3, ChartQA 68.7->68.8, SEED 71.7->71.8; SFT drops GQA to 63.5
baselines_compared: SFT (supervised fine-tuning on identical 49K data) — primary comparison
Larger generalists: Qwen2.5-VL-72B, InternVL2.5-8B/78B, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Griffon-13B, Griffon v2, Lumen
Detection specialists: Faster R-CNN-FPN, DAB-DETR, DETR, Pix2Seq, GroundingDINO
